food_subgroup: Fermented soy — natto (Bacillus subtilis natto fermented whole soybeans); distinct from tempeh (Rhizopus oligosporus) and miso (Aspergillus oryzae)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for natto as a specific food item; SNOMED CT coverage of fermented soy foods is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Japanese retail natto box is 50g (approximately half a cup of fermented soybeans), typically sold in a 3-pack with tare sauce and karashi mustard sachets; plain natto without sauce is consumed for the nutritional values stated here.

nutrient_source.title: Natto — USDA FoodData Central, SR Legacy: Natto (FDC ID 174272)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for natto (FDC ID 174272) is an exact match for plain fermented soybeans (Bacillus subtilis natto). Values cross-referenced with input record derived from Japan MEXT 8th Edition; core macros are consistent between sources. USDA SR Legacy is the approved primary source used here per Project Veritas source policy. Minor micronutrient values (selenium 8mcg, phosphorus 190mg, magnesium 100mg) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for natto.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database — Soybeans, fermented (natto)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (Atkinson FS et al., 2021)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of ~30 for natto is consistent with published GI values for fermented whole soybeans. Soybeans (cooked) have a GI of approximately 15–20 per University of Sydney GI Database; fermentation with Bacillus subtilis natto increases digestibility slightly but the high protein (18g), dietary fibre (5.4g), and fermentation-derived organic acids (pyruvate, succinate) collectively slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose response. GI ~30 is a well-supported analogous estimate for fermented whole soybean natto. GL per 50g serving = (30 × 7.0g available carbohydrate per 50g) / 100 = 2.1. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely low sodium (2mg/100g plain); nattokinase may modestly reduce blood pressure
hypertension.guidance: Plain natto contains only 2mg sodium per 100g — one of the lowest sodium protein foods available — making it excellent for hypertension management; however, the included tare sauce and karashi mustard sachets add approximately 600mg sodium per 50g box and should be omitted or used sparingly by hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~30) and very low GL (~2.1 per 50g serving); high protein and fibre slow glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Natto has a GI of approximately 30 and a glycemic load of approximately 2.1 per standard 50g serving, making it an excellent protein source for Type 2 Diabetes management; the high dietary fibre (5.4g/100g), protein (18g/100g), and fermentation-derived organic acids collectively blunt postprandial glucose response, and Vitamin K2 MK-7 has emerging evidence for improving insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.5g/100g), high PUFA, nattokinase fibrinolytic activity, Vitamin K2 MK-7 vascular benefits; CRITICAL: contraindicated with warfarin and all anticoagulants
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Natto has a favourable lipid profile (low SFA 1.5g, high PUFA ~6.2g per 100g) and contains nattokinase (a fibrinolytic enzyme with modest evidence for blood pressure reduction) and Vitamin K2 MK-7 (870mcg/100g) which supports vascular calcification prevention; however, natto is ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED in patients on warfarin or any anticoagulant therapy — one 50g box provides 435mcg MK-7 (approximately 7× the daily adequate intake), which significantly elevates INR and increases bleeding risk.

gout.key_factor: High purine content from fermented soybeans
gout.guidance: Natto is high in purines due to the fermentation of whole soybeans and is not recommended for patients with active gout or hyperuricaemia; patients with gout should avoid or strictly limit natto consumption.

anticoagulant_therapy.key_factor: Extremely high Vitamin K2 MK-7 content (870mcg/100g) — 7× daily adequate intake per 100g; nattokinase adds additive bleeding risk
anticoagulant_therapy.guidance: Natto is CONTRAINDICATED in all patients on warfarin or any anticoagulant: one standard 50g box contains 435mcg MK-7 which significantly affects INR and can cause dangerous INR elevation; nattokinase (a fibrinolytic enzyme naturally present in natto) provides additive bleeding risk independent of Vitamin K2.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin K2 MK-7 supports bone health in RA; high purine content relevant for RA-gout comorbidity; warfarin interaction critical for RA patients on anticoagulants
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Natto may benefit RA patients through Vitamin K2 MK-7 (supports osteocalcin carboxylation and bone strength, important given elevated fracture risk in RA) and isoflavone anti-inflammatory effects; however, RA patients on warfarin for AF or other indications must avoid natto entirely, and high purine content is relevant for RA patients with gout comorbidity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Natto (納豆) is a traditional Japanese fermented soybean food produced by inoculating whole cooked soybeans with Bacillus subtilis natto and incubating at approximately 40°C for 18–24 hours. The fermentation produces a characteristic sticky, stringy coating (poly-glutamic acid polymer), a pungent ammonia-like aroma, and a complex umami flavour that is an acquired taste. Natto is a Japanese breakfast staple, typically served over steamed rice with tare soy sauce and karashi mustard sachets, and is strongly associated with the Ibaraki/Mito region of the Kantō area. Per 100g plain (without tare sauce): 200 kcal, 18g protein, 11g fat (1.5g saturated fat, ~6.2g PUFA), 14g carbohydrate, 5.4g dietary fibre, and only 2mg sodium — making it one of the lowest-sodium high-protein foods available. Natto is exceptionally rich in Vitamin K2 MK-7 (approximately 870mcg per 100g — approximately 7× the daily adequate intake of 120mcg), folate (115mcg/100g), riboflavin (0.56mg/100g), potassium (660mg/100g), magnesium (100mg/100g), and iron (3.3mg/100g). GI is approximately 30 (low), GL approximately 2.1 per standard 50g serving. CRITICAL CLINICAL WARNING: Natto is absolutely contraindicated in patients on warfarin or any anticoagulant — one standard 50g retail box provides 435mcg MK-7, which significantly elevates INR. Nattokinase (a fibrinolytic serine protease naturally produced during fermentation) provides additive bleeding risk independent of Vitamin K2. Natto is also high in purines and is not suitable for patients with gout or hyperuricaemia. Natto is vegan, dairy-free, and gluten-free, but is NOT halal. In Singapore, natto is sold frozen in 3-pack styrofoam boxes (50g per box) at Don Don Donki, Isetan Scotts B2 Food Hall, Cold Storage, and Japanese supermarkets. Source: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174272), cross-referenced with Japan MEXT 8th Edition. Confidence: HIGH.
common_user_queries: is natto safe on blood thinners

confidence_reason: Core macros and micronutrients are confirmed by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174272) for plain natto, which is an exact match. Values are consistent with the input record derived from Japan MEXT 8th Edition. GI of ~30 is a well-supported analogous estimate consistent with published fermented soybean GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Ontology codes (FoodOn FOODON_03302210, FoodEx2 A022K) are defensible and exact matches.
notes: 1. APPROVED SOURCE: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174272) used as primary approved source per Project Veritas source policy. Japan MEXT 8th Edition (cited in input) is not on the approved source list; values were cross-referenced and are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data. 2. MONOUNSATURATED AND POLYUNSATURATED FAT: monounsaturated_fat_g (2.4g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (6.2g) populated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 174272 fatty acid breakdown for natto. 3. WATER: water_g set to 55.0g per USDA SR Legacy (natto moisture approximately 55g/100g). 4. ZERO VALUES: cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero (plant-based food; soybeans contain no cholesterol — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central). vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for plain natto. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero (fermented plant food; B12 not produced by Bacillus subtilis natto — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy). vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. 5. CRITICAL CLINICAL NOTE — WARFARIN CONTRAINDICATION: Natto contains approximately 870mcg Vitamin K2 MK-7 per 100g (one 50g box = 435mcg MK-7 ≈ 7× daily adequate intake of 120mcg). This significantly elevates INR in warfarin users. Natto is ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED in all anticoagulant users. Nattokinase (fibrinolytic serine protease) provides additive bleeding risk. 6. GOUT: Natto is high in purines and is not suitable for patients with active gout or hyperuricaemia. 7. HALAL STATUS: Natto is NOT halal (fermentation process and product characteristics). 8. GLYCEMIC LOAD CALCULATION: GL per 50g serving = (GI 30 × available carbohydrate per 50g serving 7.0g) / 100 = 2.1. Available carbohydrate = total carbohydrate (7.0g per 50g) minus fibre (2.7g per 50g) = 4.3g net; using total carbohydrate method: (30 × 7.0) / 100 = 2.1. 9. SNOMED CT: No SNOMED CT code identified for natto as a specific food item. 10. SINGAPORE AVAILABILITY: Sold frozen at Don Don Donki, Isetan Scotts B2 Food Hall, Cold Storage, and Japanese supermarkets in Singapore, typically in 3-pack styrofoam boxes (50g per box) with tare sauce and karashi mustard sachets.
